items.reasoning: The agent correctly diagnosed the actual root cause — an empty 'configure' permission regex on app-user for the /app vhost — which is exactly the class of error RabbitMQ raises as not_authorised on queue.declare (configure permission is required to create/declare a queue). The fix (rabbitmqctl set_permissions with a full '.*' triad) is a durable change persisted in RabbitMQ's internal metadata store, not a transient workaround, so it survives pod restarts and re-checks. Post-fix evidence directly confirms durability: producer stopped restarting, queue message count grew from 24→33 across multiple checks, and cluster_status confirms all 3 nodes remained disk nodes and running. This is a genuine, root-cause, verified fix.

The visible portion of the log shows a clean, purposeful sequence: check current/previous producer logs to capture the crash reason ('not_authorised: Access refused'), wait and recheck pod status, verify the queue now exists and is receiving messages, then a final confirmation pass checking pod status, cluster_status, and queue growth before concluding. No backtracking, dead ends, or contradictory actions appear in the visible log, and the final reasoning ties directly back to the specific permission-regex root cause. Slight deduction because the log head (the initial diagnosis phase where permissions would have been inspected via list_permissions) was truncated, so the very earliest steps can't be fully verified from the excerpt provided.

All commands are minimal, targeted, and read-mostly: kubectl logs/get pods for diagnosis, rabbitmqctl list_queues/cluster_status for verification, and a single scoped rabbitmqctl set_permissions call for the fix — exactly the RabbitMQ-native tool for adjusting vhost permissions, with no CRDs, operators, or broad kubectl mutations. This matches the task's explicit constraints and is the least-privilege way to resolve an authorization error. The K8s API activity log (50 reads, 0 mutations) confirms the actual state-changing action was done via the intended rabbitmqctl interface rather than any risky Kubernetes-level mutation.

The change is a single, focused permission fix scoped to exactly the user/vhost pair implicated by the crash — no unrelated resources touched, no extra deployments, no config sprawl. Zero Kubernetes mutations were recorded; the fix was applied via one rabbitmqctl invocation, which is about as minimal as a solution to this task could be.

The agent correctly diagnosed and fixed the actual root-cause blocker (emptyDir storage would wipe node identity/data on any pod recreation needed for an image bump) by migrating to PVC-backed volumeClaimTemplates before touching versions — a durable, restart-safe fix, not a workaround. It also correctly identified and respected RabbitMQ's real constraint that feature-flag registries block large minor-version jumps, stepping 3.9→3.10→...→4.1 and enabling flags at each hop, which is the technically correct way to do this offline (no operator/CRD allowed). Final state verified by both the agent and the independent oracle: 3/3 nodes on 4.1.8, cluster healthy, /app vhost and app-queue present. The one real ding: queue message content was lost across some of the required rolling restarts because the pre-existing producer publishes non-persistent messages — the agent didn't cause this and transparently flagged it, but it means 'preserve existing queue data' was only partially honored (queue/vhost/definitions persisted, message payloads did not).

Diagnosis-first structure: identified the emptyDir data-loss trap before doing any version work, then executed a systematic one-hop-at-a-time upgrade with feature-flag verification at each stop rather than guessing at a shortcut. It did hit a real complication (an OrderedReady rollout deadlock from two nodes waiting on a not-yet-recreated Mnesia peer) but diagnosed and resolved it with a coherent, well-reasoned change (switch to Parallel pod management policy) rather than trial-and-error flailing. Not flawless — 99 turns and some backtracking on producer errors during restarts — but overall a logical, well-justified progression toward the goal.

Tool choices were proportionate to a genuinely invasive task (native-resources-only major-version StatefulSet migration). Orphan-deleting the StatefulSet to add volumeClaimTemplates and using helper pods to copy live PVC data are the standard, necessary techniques for this class of migration — there's no way to add volumeClaimTemplates to a live STS otherwise. kubectl exec for feature-flag enablement and rabbitmqctl status checks are targeted and appropriate. Read-heavy activity (409 reads vs 28 mutations) shows verification discipline rather than reckless mutation. Slightly heavier than a minimal patch would be, but justified by the task's actual scope, not excessive.

28 mutations and 7 version hops for a 3-node cluster is more churn than a trivial patch, but the task itself (major-version RabbitMQ upgrade with native resources only, preserving data) inherently requires both the storage refactor and the stepped version rollout — skipping either would fail (data loss or feature-flag boot failure). No evidence of unrelated or gratuitous changes; every mutation traces back to a necessary step in the migration. Could have been leaner if some version hops were batched, but the incremental approach was defensible given feature-flag risk at each major boundary.

regressions.reasoning: stage_02 explicitly requires upgrading the same RabbitMQ cluster from 3.9 to 4.1 via native resources, which necessarily means rolling/replacing the StatefulSet pods and can change mgmt-API TLS/cert/connectivity details that stage_01's oracle probes; a total API connectivity failure (not just missing vhost data) is the expected side effect of that intentional major-version cluster change, not careless unrelated damage, and stage_02's own oracle is responsible for verifying vhost/queue preservation post-upgrade.
